story:
The day I swallowed truth was an ordinary Thursday in April. I wasn’t ready. My mouth went dry, parted, and the truth slid in. I gulped it down like a stone. It did not vanish. It sank and clung, black and heavy. I told myself secrets can’t hurt you, that they live only in the mind. But even as I argued with myself, I felt something inside me stiffen.

I had been leaving my mother’s retirement community when it happened. First, the screech. Then the thump. A cloud of smoke rose a block away. I wished it would stay a cloud and keep the scene hidden, but it thinned, and I saw. A body on the curb. And a boy at the wheel. He was not a man, not really. I knew him. I was his ninth-grade science teacher. Parker Sumter.

He didn’t see me. He didn’t see anyone. He drove away, leaving the body and a new cloud that, too, abandoned me. For a moment I told myself I could be wrong. His face had looked wrong in the shock, warped by fear. Maybe it wasn’t him. But a small candle of memory flickered: the Sumter Farm Harvest Festival, hayrides when I was almost a teen, that exact truck. The sweet turned sour. Knowing punished me.

I called the police. The victim was Mr. Bradshaw, an old man with dementia who sometimes wandered. “Hit and run,” the officers said. Lady Justice, why put a stone in my hand and tell me to throw? One life was gone. Was I meant to end another? I told myself I couldn’t. I kept the stone instead. “Yes, officer, I only heard it. By the time I looked, the car was gone.”

My mother found me in the crowd. “I’m okay,” I told her. That part wasn’t a lie. But the lie came anyway, and it was worse to lie to a person I loved than to a system with no face. In that moment I realized something: my inside was no longer like hers. It was straw. I went back to my classroom, and Parker came and went, and then he graduated. I told no one.

Six years have passed since Mr. Bradshaw died. The shaking stopped. The sense of being watched has faded. I avoided liquor. When the numbness of not-feeling grew too heavy, I sometimes used drugs, not to drift but to jerk awake—an unwise crutch I am trying to abandon. I am mostly okay. Better, even. But alone in this secret.

I still have a dream. The sky is blank and gray, an autumn evening. The Harvest Festival again. I am driving the hayride. My students sit on the bales. I don’t see their faces, but I know they’re mine. Suddenly I understand I am still a kid. I whisper, “Can you keep a secret?” We giggle. Then panic. I don’t know how to drive. I try to warn them, but hay fills my mouth. My body locks. We crash—into a tree, into a ditch—one of those ends. The children die. I do not. I lie on the dirt, unable to move. I watch. I am a scarecrow, stuffed with straw, guarding a field I cannot save.
